narrative:
Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney concluded a visit to India from February 27 to March 2, 2026, marking a significant reset in bilateral relations that had deteriorated following diplomatic tensions over the 2023 assassination of Sikh separatist Hardeep Singh Nijjar [1][2]. The visit resulted in multiple agreements between the two nations, with both leaders emphasizing renewed partnership and cooperation.

## Key Agreements and Cooperation Areas

The centerpiece of the visit was a 10-year nuclear energy deal described as "landmark" by Modi, covering long-term uranium supply and cooperation on small modular reactors and advanced reactors [2]. Beyond nuclear energy, the two countries signed agreements covering critical minerals, technology, space, defense, and education [1][2]. Both leaders also committed to concluding a comprehensive economic partnership agreement (free trade deal) by the end of 2026, with both nations seeking to reduce exposure to punitive US trade tariffs [2].

## Diplomatic Context and Tensions

The visit represents a cautious rebuilding of relations that had "almost came to a standstill" following former Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's 2023 accusation that India was linked to Nijjar's assassination on Canadian soil [2]. India "vociferously rejected" these allegations [2]. The diplomatic crisis had resulted in both countries expelling each other's diplomats and cancelling visa services [2].

However, significant tensions remain beneath the surface. Canada's spy agency continues to list India among countries carrying out espionage and foreign interference, alongside Russia, China and Iran [2]. The Canadian Security Intelligence Service stated Monday that its "threat assessment of the main perpetrators of foreign interference and espionage against Canada has not changed" [2]. Additionally, some Canadian officials, including a Liberal MP from Carney's own party and members of the Sikh diaspora, maintain that they continue to be targeted by India [2].

## Official Responses and Contradictions

Canadian Foreign Minister Anita Anand distanced herself from comments by a senior Canadian official claiming India had ceased all foreign interference in Canada, stating "The words of the senior official are not words that I personally would use" [2]. Despite this, Anand defended the re-engagement, arguing that "we need to have these diplomatic conversations in order to make progress" [2].

Carney's office cancelled a planned news conference following his meeting with Modi, citing his tight schedule [2]. The case involving four men charged over Nijjar's killing remains before the courts [2].

## Leaders' Public Statements

Modi responded positively to Carney's characterization of the visit, writing on X: "Your visit has built a strong foundation for a truly renewed partnership. I look forward to working together with you to build a future defined by trust, growth, innovation and shared prosperity for the people of our nations" [1]. Carney had described his visit as "not just a renewal of the Canada-India partnership but an expansion with new ambition and focus, between two confident countries charting their own course for the future" [1].

citations.title: Canada PM's visit to India built strong foundation for truly renewed partnership: Modi

citations.title: India and Canada reset ties with 'landmark' nuclear energy deal
